item.content:encoded: dado is funny when he acts a contrast to a serious character. It doesn't work when the character is wacky like this one. At least make them wack in a weird way and not just like, a weird person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is late, but the pictured D-Class is not holding a caplet, they are holding a tablet.

item.title: Re: Image Use and Notes
item.content:encoded:
Notes
Notes

Apparently people who just say "I want my head to stop hurting" and then keep their mouths shut until the pills arrive.

(Why are we whispering?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every dado article reads the same to me. They read like that "you want dog" article. Except the doggo that makes Sausage Pets in that literal puppy mill is somehow both more competent at running a business and a better listener than dado.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you read it after December 6 2018 then you read it with way, way less footnotes. :)

In other SCP articles I've never had trouble ignoring or processing an order of magnitude more footnotes, but obviously mileage varies.

Glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, my own migraine experience was definitely a major inspiration here. My eyes really feel like they're too big for my sockets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think i mostly have the same praises and problems as everyone else but i found the footnotes not hard to ignore which might be just me, so the entertainment of dado took prominence over the few issues

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You get an upvote here entirely because this hit weirdly close to home for me as I sit here reading SCP stuff on my phone while recovering from a migraine and being entirely too tempted by sketchy migraine treatments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank, pls leave feedback on amazon prime also :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
dado b everywher now

2 much dado, but ok skip. upvot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure, why not? I thought it might have been a mistake when I did it. And at least you're not complaining about insufficient clinical tone. :)

Edit: And done, to the extent I'm willing. TIL people have difficulty reading things without clicking on / hovering over links as they go, like footnotes and crosslinks. I usually read an article and look at footnotes and crosslinks later, but evidence suggests that my experience is anomalous. ;-)

Don't get me wrong: I honestly appreciate the feedback, which is why I acted on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The amount of crosslinks is annoying and unnecessary. I'd recommend removing all except the dado hub and energy drink article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with you, but we're in the minority. I excised a couple more but I'm done at this point; everything that's left is information that I don't want to lose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I like the footnotes. It's a good way to squeeze pertinent information about a specific topic into a small amount of space that is entirely optional to read.

I say keep 'em as-is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup! I'll see what else I can excise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It sounds like you cut back on footnotes already but they're still way too much. Really made it difficult to read. I like most of it beyond that though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now my head cannon voice is Zach from PhychicPebbles, Thanks!(?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm aware. They correlate in this case. The majority of dado articles are from that time. shrug

I mean, it seems weird to me to complain that I'm writing dado like he is on the dado hub, but obviously YMMV, which is fine. I like the recent dado articles too! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, he means old dado like the dado articles released earlier this year, not old dado from an in-universe perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're not the only person who suggested clipping the footnotes. I can do that right now. I guess I'm the only person who reads an article without the footnotes and then goes back and looks at them later.

As for the old dado… Well, it is set in 2015, before a lot of the other dado articles. shrug

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Yeah, I admit the footnotes are mainly for my own entertainment to some degree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The clinical tone is just something that mostly comes with time. Applying paper-writing skills from Uni is very helpful in this.
In regards to the fragmentation, its mostly the footnotes. The footnotes rival the length of the article and there are 24(!!!!) in such a short article, which really put me off. Imagine having to stop reading a book to read another book with supplementary (and not always necessary) information before going back to what you were doing. It severely bogs down the experience.
My issue with your use of dado is that it's a return to the old dado which the site has largely moved away from for the better, at least in my opinion. Or at least this was the case until the last 24 hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, there were like 3 less when I first thought of this, but that's what I get for being slow to act. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like your your headcanon. :)

There is no canon. Really, this is not my own headcanon for what dado looks like; it really is what PoI Clayton imagined he looked like while hallucinating. After all, she never met him. It's supposed to be more indicative of her mental state than anything else.

It's a minor change, but would it make a difference if there was a question mark at the end of the post it note?

"yes i am dado?"

Edit: Went ahead and changed that bit, I like the idea that even she isn't sure if that depiction is correct. Way I see it, even people in the universe are allowed to have dado headcanons. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
nods appreciatively Fair!

My headcanon is that he actually does look like a regular old non-anomalous human and originally was one. But dado, even in his original life, was gifted with a rare breed of mental tenacity which allowed him to stumble into things beyond the ken of mortals without it altering his baseline personality in the slightest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would happily accept suggestions on fixing the spotty clinical tone! I find it interesting that you find dado to be shoehorned, when the idea came to me when reading the dado articles, but that's probably the effect of the fragmentation you reference, which I'd also happily accept advice on.

-Kirt

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I prefer not having any idea what dado's "true form" might look like

I GREATLY agree with this, despite having my own headcanon for what dado looks like (in the collapsible below)

+ show block
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought the statue bit at the end was a little much. Even with an artist's rendition - which is implied to be at least partly hallucinated - I prefer not having any idea what dado's "true form" might look like (when he doesn't have the appearance of LordStonefish).

However, the hallucination effects were interestingly creepy, and the artist's enthusiasm for reviews and believable writing voice compensated for the now-familiar "comic wordplay misunderstanding" dado trope. Plus the pill, unusually, does the exact thing it's supposed to do if taken as recommended.

That said, what finally pushed it into upvote territory for me was how perfectly it would fit in that one scene in my Hitchhiker's Guide crossover tale. I freely admit my bias. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, no, I love dado articles. It's just kind of a flood at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is way too fragmented for me to enjoy reading. That paired with the spotty clinical tone, cliche 'crazy until dead' effect, shoehorning of dado, and the footnotes make this a subpar experience.

item.title: is it really?
item.content:encoded:
it's the one, the only: DADO

I love dado help

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too many footnotes, however it’s a good read +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've had this in my head since I read my first few dado articles. I hope it's not going to get downvoted just because it's a dado article.

item.title: Image Use and Notes
item.content:encoded:
Playboy bunny pill image is borrowed from:

Original Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Playboy_Bunny.jpg
Creator: Psychonaught

Original work was released into the public domain.

The final image, the post-it note, is mine, but I hereby release it into the public domain as well, for what it's worth.

+ Notes
- Notes

Obviously, this SCP owes a lot to SCP-3929. It also comes from my own struggles with migraines (no joke, I got a migraine while writing it) and from one thing that always puzzled me about dado: Who is recommending dado to these people who end up with disastrous pharmaceuticals from him?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Godammit another dado article
